title: Planning for Dialectical Abstinence Plan for Abstinence  Â‰ 1. Enjoy your success, but with a clear mind; plan for temptations to relapse.

From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission

Distress Tolerance Handout 17a   (Distress Tolerance Worksheet 14)
Planning for Dialectical Abstinence
Plan for Abstinence

Â‰   1.	 Enjoy your success, but with a clear mind; plan for temptations to relapse.

Â‰   2.	Spend time or touch base with people who will reinforce you for abstinence.

Â‰   3.	Plan reinforcing activities to do instead of addictive behaviors.

Â‰   4.	Burn bridges: Avoid cues and high-risk situations for addictive behaviors.

Â‰   5.	Build new bridges: Develop images, smells, and mental activities (such as,
urge surfing) to compete with information associated with craving.

Â‰   6.	Find alternative ways to rebel.

Â‰   7.	 Publicly announce abstinence; deny any idea of lapsing to addiction.
Plan for Harm Reduction

Â‰   1.	 Call your therapist, sponsor, or mentor for skills coaching.

Â‰   2.	Get in contact with other effective people who can help.

Â‰   3.	Get rid of temptations; surround yourself with cues for effective behaviors.

Â‰   4.	Review skills and handouts from DBT.

Â‰   5.	Opposite action (Emotion Regulation Handout 10) can be rehearsed
to fight guilt and shame. If no other option works, go to an anonymous
meeting of any sort and publicly report your lapse.

Â‰   6.	Building mastery and coping ahead for emotional situations (Emotion
Regulation Handout 19), and checking the facts (Emotion Regulation
Handout 8), can be used to fight feelings of being out of control.

Â‰   7.	 Interpersonal skills (Interpersonal Effectiveness Handouts 5â€“7), such as
asking for help from family, friends, sponsors, ministers, or counselors,
can also be helpful. If you are isolated, help can often be found via online
support groups.

Â‰   8.	Conduct a chain analysis to analyze what prompted the lapse (General
Handouts 7, 7a).

Â‰   9.	Problem-solve right away to find a way to â€śget back on the wagonâ€ť and
repair any damage you have done (Emotion Regulation Handout 12).

Â‰ 10.	 Distract yourself, self-soothe, and improve the moment.

Â‰ 11.	 Cheerlead yourself.

Â‰ 12.	Do pros and cons of stopping addictive behaviors (Distress Tolerance
Handout 5).

Â‰ 13.	Stay away from extreme thinking. Donâ€™t let one slip turn into a disaster.

Â‰ 14.	 Recommit to 100% total abstinence.

Distress Tolerance Handout 17a   (Distress Tolerance Worksheet 14)
Planning for Dialectical Abstinence
Plan for Abstinence

Â‰   1.	 Enjoy your success, but with a clear mind; plan for temptations to relapse.

Â‰   2.	Spend time or touch base with people who will reinforce you for abstinence.

Â‰   3.	Plan reinforcing activities to do instead of addictive behaviors.

Â‰   4.	Burn bridges: Avoid cues and high-risk situations for addictive behaviors.

Â‰   5.	Build new bridges: Develop images, smells, and mental activities (such as,
urge surfing) to compete with information associated with craving.

Â‰   6.	Find alternative ways to rebel.

Â‰   7.	 Publicly announce abstinence; deny any idea of lapsing to addiction.
Plan for Harm Reduction

Â‰   1.	 Call your therapist, sponsor, or mentor for skills coaching.

Â‰   2.	Get in contact with other effective people who can help.

Â‰   3.	Get rid of temptations; surround yourself with cues for effective behaviors.

Â‰   4.	Review skills and handouts from DBT.

Â‰   5.	Opposite action (Emotion Regulation Handout 10) can be rehearsed
to fight guilt and shame. If no other option works, go to an anonymous
meeting of any sort and publicly report your lapse.

Â‰   6.	Building mastery and coping ahead for emotional situations (Emotion
Regulation Handout 19), and checking the facts (Emotion Regulation
Handout 8), can be used to fight feelings of being out of control.

Â‰   7.	 Interpersonal skills (Interpersonal Effectiveness Handouts 5â€“7), such as
asking for help from family, friends, sponsors, ministers, or counselors,
can also be helpful. If you are isolated, help can often be found via online
support groups.

Â‰   8.	Conduct a chain analysis to analyze what prompted the lapse (General
Handouts 7, 7a).

Â‰   9.	Problem-solve right away to find a way to â€śget back on the wagonâ€ť and
repair any damage you have done (Emotion Regulation Handout 12).

Â‰ 10.	 Distract yourself, self-soothe, and improve the moment.

Â‰ 11.	 Cheerlead yourself.

Â‰ 12.	Do pros and cons of stopping addictive behaviors (Distress Tolerance
Handout 5).

Â‰ 13.	Stay away from extreme thinking. Donâ€™t let one slip turn into a disaster.

Â‰ 14.	 Recommit to 100% total abstinence.

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Planning for Dialectical Abstinence Plan for Abstinence  Â‰ 1. Enjoy your success, but with a clear mind; plan for temptations to relapse.
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Distress tolerance means getting through the wave without adding harm. Use gentle, medically safer versions first.
tiny_steps: Choose one body-safe skill for 30 seconds.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Replace intense exercise with hand squeeze, paced exhale, safe fidget, weighted comfort if tolerated, or visual grounding.
Avoid cold shock/breath-holding/intense exercise unless already clinician-approved and safe that day.
Keep oxygen, rescue meds, allergy plan, mobility aids, and exit routes accessible.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use crisis skills as punishment, sensory flooding, or exposure without consent.
